skill.name: systematic-debugging
skill.description: Guides agents through root-cause debugging before changing code, with tracing, evidence gathering, hypothesis testing, and verification steps.
skill.summary: Follow a structured root-cause process before fixing bugs.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported 126 potential issues, but manual review found most high-severity matches are false positives from markdown examples, diagrams, timeout variable names, and TypeScript template strings. The confirmed risks are legitimate local debugging behaviors: a shell helper that runs project tests, examples that inspect environment or keychain state, and filesystem polling guidance. No evidence found of malicious intent, prompt injection, persistence, network exfiltration, or credential theft.

medium_findings.title: Local Test Runner Script Executes Project Commands
medium_findings.description: The bundled find-polluter.sh helper enumerates test files and runs npm test for each match. This is legitimate debugging automation, but it executes project scripts and should only be used in trusted repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script directly runs npm test on discovered files, confirming local command execution. Its purpose is transparent and local, with no evidence of exfiltration or persistence.

medium_findings.title: Diagnostic Examples Inspect Local Environment State
medium_findings.description: The skill includes examples that print environment variables, list macOS keychains, inspect signing identities, read process.env.NODE_ENV, and report cwd. These examples are useful for debugging but can expose sensitive local context if copied into shared logs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples explicitly inspect environment and keychain state. They do not extract secrets or send data externally, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Polling and Pollution Checks
low_findings.description: The documentation and helper script check for file existence and list matched paths while debugging test pollution. This is expected for the skill, but users should avoid broad or sensitive path targets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem operations are explicit and limited to local checks. They are not hidden, destructive, or networked, but they can reveal path metadata.

low_findings.title: Static Pattern Matches in Documentation Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: Most external command and weak-cryptography alerts are caused by fenced markdown diagrams, template strings, timeoutMs identifiers, and explanatory examples. Review found no prompt injection, unauthorized network access, persistence, or credential exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines were reviewed in context and are documentation or ordinary TypeScript error messages. The only confirmed executable file is the local test helper script.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Test Runner Script Executes Project Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The bundled find-polluter.sh helper enumerates test files and runs npm test for each match. This is legitimate debugging automation, but it executes project scripts and should only be used in trusted repositories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The script directly runs npm test on discovered files, confirming local command execution. Its purpose is transparent and local, with no evidence of exfiltration or persistence.

dangerous_patterns.title: Diagnostic Examples Inspect Local Environment State
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes examples that print environment variables, list macOS keychains, inspect signing identities, read process.env.NODE_ENV, and report cwd. These examples are useful for debugging but can expose sensitive local context if copied into shared logs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples explicitly inspect environment and keychain state. They do not extract secrets or send data externally, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem Polling and Pollution Checks
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation and helper script check for file existence and list matched paths while debugging test pollution. This is expected for the skill, but users should avoid broad or sensitive path targets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem operations are explicit and limited to local checks. They are not hidden, destructive, or networked, but they can reveal path metadata.

content.user_title: Debug Bugs With Root Cause Discipline
content.value_statement: Quick fixes often hide the real cause and create repeat failures. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured process for evidence-first debugging.
actual_capabilities: Requires root cause investigation before proposing fixes.
Guides reproduction, error review, recent-change checks, and evidence gathering.
Uses backward tracing to find where bad data originated.
Promotes single-hypothesis testing with minimal changes.
Provides condition-based waiting guidance for flaky asynchronous tests.
Includes a shell helper for finding tests that create unwanted files.
limitations: It does not automatically inspect a repository unless the agent has tool access.
It cannot prove a root cause without reliable evidence from the target system.
Some examples run local commands and should be used only in trusted projects.
It may slow very small fixes because it prioritizes investigation first.

use_cases.title: Diagnose failing tests
use_cases.description: Trace a failure from symptom to source before editing test or product code.

use_cases.title: Reduce flaky async behavior
use_cases.description: Replace guessed sleeps with waits for real conditions and clear timeouts.

use_cases.title: Handle production incidents
use_cases.description: Gather evidence across system boundaries before applying a targeted fix.

prompt_templates.title: Start a bug investigation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use systematic debugging. Review this failure, identify what evidence we need, and do not suggest a fix until the root cause is clear.

prompt_templates.title: Trace a failing test
prompt_templates.prompt: Use systematic debugging on this test failure. Reproduce the symptom, check recent changes, and trace where the wrong value first appears.

prompt_templates.title: Debug a multi-layer issue
prompt_templates.prompt: Use systematic debugging across these components. Add diagnostic checkpoints for each boundary and explain which layer first diverges from expected behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Recover after failed fixes
prompt_templates.prompt: We tried several fixes and the issue remains. Use systematic debugging to reassess the architecture, list failed hypotheses, and define the next evidence test.

output_examples.input: A test sometimes receives pending status instead of completed status.
output: Phase 1: reproduce the failure and capture timing evidence.
Trace where the status changes from completed to pending.
Do not add more sleep time until the state transition is understood.

output_examples.input: A build fails only in CI after a signing step.
output: Check each boundary: workflow secrets, build script environment, signing configuration, and final command invocation.
Identify the first boundary where expected data is missing.
Form one hypothesis and test it with the smallest diagnostic change.

output_examples.input: A local test creates an unexpected .git directory.
output: Trace the directory value backward through the call chain.
Find where an empty or wrong path enters the workflow.
Fix the source and add validation at each layer that accepts the path.

best_practices: Collect evidence before proposing code changes.
Test one hypothesis at a time with the smallest useful change.
Document the root cause and the verification that proves the fix worked.
anti_patterns: Adding quick fixes before understanding the failure path.
Combining several changes so the true fix cannot be isolated.
Using arbitrary sleeps to hide race conditions.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It guides an AI assistant through a structured root-cause debugging process before making fixes.

faq.question: Does it write code automatically?
faq.answer: It can guide code changes, but only after the issue is reproduced, traced, and explained.

faq.question: Can it help with flaky tests?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes condition-based waiting guidance for replacing guessed delays with explicit checks.

faq.question: Is it safe to use in production incidents?
faq.answer: Yes, but command examples should be reviewed before use because they can print environment or system state.

faq.question: Why does it discourage quick fixes?
faq.answer: Quick fixes often treat symptoms and can create repeated failures or new defects.

faq.question: What tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
